title: 69 Pre Moon Speedmaster with applied logo dial
description: If you’re looking for an affordable pre-moon Speedmaster with all the vintage feel and DNA, this is probably it.  Fitted with the correct “Seamonster” caseback, this ref. 145.022-69 features an earlier ’68 step dial with an applied logo—likely swapped during a service, as anything was possible during this transition period when the 321 gave way to the pre-moon 861 Speedmaster.  Very reasonably priced for this reason, here’s a very cool Speedy that can easily become your reliable daily wearer, wi

title: Tropical Polerouter Date
description: Nice tropical Polerouters are hard to find as the white wolf.   Here’s a ref. 204607-1, fully overhauled and featuring an evenly tropicalized dial. A true wabi-sabi Polerouter, where tiny imperfections add depth, charm, and character.  The dial color is simply stunning under the NOS signed plexi. A charismatic Polerouter, a perfect summer watch and the thrill of a genuine tropical Polerouter—without breaking the bank.

title: Polerouter sub 869122 with blue bezel
description: Probably the only diver powered by a micro-rotor, the asymmetric Polerouter Sub was produced with several bezel variations. Referenced by Universal Genève from 869120 to 869124, this example features the blue “Bakelite” bezel of ref. 869122.  The dial, hands, and bezel have aged in perfect harmony, while the case remains exceptionally sharp—remarkable for a 60-year-old tool watch. The vibrant blue bezel, paired with red dial accents and orange hour and seconds hands, gives the watch a funky edge

title: Early 35,5mm Polerouter Date
description: The recent reissue of the Polerouter by the reborn Universal Genève is setting clearer benchmarks in terms of value for the vintage models. It is also reinforcing the importance of authenticity and a deeper understanding of each reference. Rarity, condition, dial variations, parts and correct configurations… Each detail has it’s importance and what was once a niche field has become a more defined and informed space for collectors.  Here is a very nice 204503-2, one of the very first Polerouter d

title: Speedmaster 321 with rare B4 transitional dial
description: Here we love transitional watches. When designs are slowly changing. This 145.012-67 has all the DNA of a classic Moonwatch,  starting with a real 321 caliber and a DON bezel. The originality comes from its rare B4 transitional dial. It still keeps the applied logo, but the markers are shorter than a classic 145.012. The drop second has already turned into a alpha second. The moment when a 321 is slowly mutating  to a 69 …   if you are a speedy geek looking for a rare reference, or just want to

title: Omega Flightmaster Mk1 « No Dots » Ref.145.013
description: Primitive designs are always the best. Here is the first iteration of the Flightmaster made in 1969. Nick named the « No Dots » because of absence of the two dots at the 12 o’clock marker. Very specific to the very early model, the other main difference is the 24h night and day green sub dial at 9 o’clock.   This is a big watch from the 70s. A water mark period for sophisticated and funky design tool watches.  The dial and hands of this one have nicely faded to a very smooth color way.   A watch

title: Seamaster 300 Ref. ST 165.024 (1966 – Early Version)
description: A cornerstone of Omega’s legendary 1957 Trilogy, the Seamaster 300 is an icon of dive watch design — a model that evolved continuously throughout a decade. From the CK to the ST series, its rich history and countless variations make it one of the most fascinating and collectible watches of its era. The upcoming release of the Seamaster Only book will shine a renewed light on this historic model, restoring its well-deserved prestige and bringing it back to the forefront of collectors’ attention.

title: 1984 Speedmaster with rare Télémètre bezel
description: From the 1970s through the 1990s, the ref. 145.022 remained remarkably consistent, evolving only subtly over nearly two decades. Sitting right in the middle of this long production run, this 1984 Speedmaster is often regarded as one of the best-value vintage Speedmasters available today. More affordable than a new one, it offers a compelling blend of vintage charm and everyday reliability.  This particular example is in very good condition, featuring an unpolished case and retaining its original

title: Datejust 16013 2 tones gilt dial
description: Probably the most iconic Datejust seen in 80s Hong Kong gangster movies — the Rolex Datejust ref. 16013 in its unmistakable two-tone configuration, here fitted with a gilt glossy black dial.  The watch is, of course, in pristine condition, paired with its signature Jubilee bracelet. The glossy dial is immaculate, exactly as it should be.  It comes complete with its original papers, confirming its provenance from… Hong Kong — where else?

title: Uni-Compax ref. 884100/02 — aka “Big Eye”
description: The rarest of the Sport UG Compax family. Most serious research points to fewer than 300–400 pieces produced per dial color, with probably less than 30 examples known today across both configurations. The white dial 884100/02 stands as the rarer of the two, with only a handful of documented pieces in collector circles.  This example is one of those few survivors. Not mint — and that’s exactly the point. Everything has aged with gracefully with remarkable balance. The red crosshair has naturally

title: Tri-Compax Evil Clapton ref 881101/02
description: Long before the Polerouter, Universal Genève built its reputation on a complication whose name became synonymous with the brand itself. Launched in 1944, the Tri-Compax was the first chronograph to feature a full calendar, quickly becoming one of the most complex wristwatches of its era.  Reissued in the 1960s in a sportier version, it achieved true icon status thanks to its frequent appearances on the wrist of legendary musician Eric Clapton.  The so-called “evil” version, with its black dial a

title: Early 69 chocolate serial range Speedmaster
description: Here is an exceptional tropical ’69.  Its nobility comes from its serial number, sitting perfectly within the official “chocolate” range. Being among the early ’69s, it also inherits a DON bezel from the calibre 321 Speedmasters.  And what a bezel it is. Beautifully faded over the years, it now pairs perfectly with the chocolate dial.  Speaking of the dial, this is probably one of the nicest chocolate dials I have come across. The tropical tone is rich and intense, yet soft and evenly distribute

title: Blancpain Fifty Fathoms Rotomatic Incabloc
description: The Fifty Fathoms is a legend. And this one is an affordable grail. The condition is second to none. A dressy museum grade Blancpain Fifty Fathoms. Sharp and unmolested like the very first days. The 34mm Rotomatic was the « amateur » recreational version of the professional diver Fifty Fathoms. They both appeared all together in the early fifties catalog.  In a more dressy case, the watch is also powered by the automatic AS 1361N, like the big ones. The pristine dial is glossy with applied marke

title: Tropical Gilt Polerouter ref 204503-2
description: The wabi-sabi of tropical dials is inherently unpredictable.  Here we have a NO-LUME Polerouter, reference 204503-2, one of the earlier Polerouter references featuring a date window. This reference is easily identified by its original round bubble loupe (no trapeze) and the early “shield” UG logo on the dial.  While non-radium dials are usually expected to tropicalize more evenly, this example truly stands apart, displaying an exceptional gradient ranging from caramel to dark coffee-bean tones.

title: Chronograph Jardur Bezelmeter 960
description: Here is a 1940’s pilote watch with a unique degrees scale on the dial.  It’s allows the pilot to calculate the duration to achieve a certain degrees turn at a constant degree angle. At 3° per second, the pilot would reach 90° right after the central chronograph hand passed the 30-second mark.  We Love this black glossy dial with the red accents. Sized at 38mm, the watch has a unique presence on the wrist. Keep in mind that this Jardur has more than 80 years. Now look at theses faceted lugs, shar

title: Tropical Speedmaster 145.022-69 with Chocolate dial
description: Here is a very nice tropical 69. Wearing a serial in the 29M range, exactly  in the famous « chocolate » range. Being among the early 69, it also inherits a DON bezel from the 321 Speedies. The tropical « chocolate » dial has a very nice mellow and evenly distributed color. The brown hue can be visible from any angle and NOT only in extreme sunlight. The lume has survived to the tropical weather and match the dial nicely.  The off white hand sets has also developed a very nice patina, very consi

title: Speedmaster 145.022-69 chocolate + Straight Writing Apollo 11
description: A 1969 Speedmaster with a chocolate dial is already rare in itself. But when it also features a ‘Straight Writing’ caseback, further complemented by the ‘Apollo 11’ engraving, you are looking at something truly exceptional.  This 145.022-69 bears a 31M serial number, dating it to 1972. After the Moon landing, Omega decided to modify the Speedmaster caseback to celebrate the Apollo 11 achievement. Before introducing the definitive engraving we all know today. During a transitional period, a so-ca

title: Mulco Antimagnetic chronograph with Spillmann case
description: Something we don’t see very often. Here is a late-1940s oversized Mulco Antimagnetic chronograph.  This watch became notable for its iconic 38 mm C.R. Spillmann case, featuring sharp angular lugs and inverted pushers. Spillmann was renowned for its case production for Rolex (most famously the Oyster case) and later for Omega (the CRS case).  At a time when most watches were produced in chrome-plated metal, Spillmann stood apart by introducing a uniquely robust stainless-steel case, significantly

title: 1961 Datejust 1603 with bamboo bezel
description: Here is a Datejust you don’t see everyday. Commonly called « Bamboo bezel » because of the pattern of its steel bezel, this rare Datejust was produced only in a very few quantity in the early 60s. Produced only for the Italian market, the bamboo bezel is very different from the fluted or the engine turned bezel. It gives to the watch a totally different look and feel. Mostly when matched with this no lume sun brushed dial, the dauphine hands and applied spear markers.  This is an early Datejust

title: Space Compax Mk2 « all black » ref 885104-2
description: Introduced as part of Universal Genève’s professional chronograph line, the Space Compax was produced in two distinct dial configurations. The first generation displays a reverse panda layout with a prominent “Big 12” numeral, while the second — the Mk2 — features a striking “all-black” dial with straight luminous hour markers framed by crisp white U-shaped surrounds.  Blending space-age design with genuine underwater capability, the Space Compax was even shortlisted for NASA’s qualification tes

title: Speedmaster Quartz LCD ref. ST 186.0004
description: Speedmaster geek? This is the only LCD watch you need in your collection. Produced for just 18 months starting in 1977, the “Speedy LCD” is probably the only digital Omega ever housed in a classic lyre-lug case with a ghost bezel.  At the time, these quartz Speedys represented cutting-edge technology. The Caliber 1620 was a high-grade quartz movement, accurate to ±0.15 seconds per day (around 5 seconds per month). Omega even priced it above the mechanical Moonwatch — $400 versus $250 for the Cal
